data.title: Tariff Tailwinds Vs. Political Headwinds: Commercial Metals Remains A Hold For Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Commercial Metals Company (NYSE: CMC) stands to benefit from significant tailwinds from infrastructure investments in highway construction, data center buildouts, LNG terminal builds, and defense spending increases in Europe. Efficiency gains from its TAG initiative appear to be supporting higher normalized through-the-cycle margins. Uncertainty surrounding the U.S. political landscape, infrastructure project funding, and economic growth has only amplified in recent months, but green shoots continue to emerge with the outlook in Europe potentially improving and CMC’s downstream pipeline growing by 10% sequentially in Q2.

I appreciate CMC’s growing pipeline of project awards and optimism for long term demand tailwinds, but see continued reason to remain cautious for the time being due to elevated macroeconomic risk and tariff uncertainty.

Much like in my last article on CMC, I want to see margins improve in the near term, but I now have seen enough to believe in the higher structural margin thesis, given the company’s skillful management of expenses in Q2. I still want to make sure the steel cycle is closer to the bottom than the top before I make an investment in CMC, but evidence is building that it may be.

Over the last ten years, CMC has delivered better total returns to shareholders than the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust ( SPY), my proxy for the S&P 500, as soon as I see that the market believes this will continue I will consider opening a position in CMC.

I rate CMC a hold and will be looking for a low-risk entry point before buying. Fundamentals will drive most of my decision, while basic technical analysis will be used to support it.

Talking Tariffs

Since May 30 th, when President Trump announced that he would double steel tariffs to 50%, the steel trade has reemerged; however, Steel Dynamics (STLD) and Nucor (NUE) have outperformed CMC. In this article, I will discuss why and try to determine whether CMC is a good bet for long-term investors at today’s price, and offer up some pros and cons of 50% tariffs.

Let’s get the basics out of the way. Import tariffs are meant to limit imports, and incentivize increased domestic production through increased price of the underlying commodity. Domestic producers earn more, while consumers end up paying more. If this spurs job growth and protects national producers it may be a worthwhile cause for the nation implementing them.

In a simple system, this works very well. However, a global and a national economy are far from simple.

First, why have tariffs boosted U.S. steel stocks? The answer lies in the potential for increased metal spreads, or steel selling prices above scrap metal prices. This spread could increase if imports of steel products are slowed or halted, and supply and demand dynamics become more favorable for U.S. producers. This would lead to an increase in steel selling prices and enable greater margins. Some analysts believe the initial price shock from a 50% tariff will come from a panic-like restocking environment, but might fade due to macroeconomic uncertainty and seasonal demand.

Another impact of high tariffs could come from inventory builds, or companies front-running the tariffs by stockpiling products and materials in advance of the tariffs. Because the recently announced tariffs went into effect on Wednesday, June 4 th, most of the front-running of tariffs will show up in CMC’s upcoming earnings report for the period ending May 31 st, 2025, with the potential for some lingering purchase activity in the next quarter if full price increases are not baked in immediately.

Let’s dive into some of these differences as well as the potential risks and rewards of the tariff trade in U.S. steel producers.

The Bigger Tariff Winner Is Steel Dynamics

Tariffs are a rising tide, but some boats sit closer to shore. STLD appears to have docked already and rallied 11% upon the announcement, while CMC was up 8% on the day. Since then, sentiment has come back to earth somewhat and both STLD and NUE have pared gains more than CMC has.

Tariffs could fatten margins for CMC and its steel-producing peers. However, the scope of the benefit from potential tariffs would impact each U.S. steel producer differently, which is what we have seen recently, with two steel manufacturing stocks that I follow, STLD and CMC, moving differently since liberation day. STLD initially doubled the return of CMC after the announcement of a 50% steel tariff, but has given back some of the edge since. If this move is justified at all, then STLD’s greater increase is warranted thanks to its business model, growth profile, and regional mix relative to CMC’s.

After liberation day, I reopened a position in STLD because the company is more exposed to flat-rolled U.S. steel production and the downstream market than CMC. STLD also has new flat-rolled capacity coming on board with its new mill in Texas. Flat-rolled steel is more exposed to competition from importers, making the combination of its current production and expanding capacity particularly attractive in a protectionist environment. High Tariffs would shield STLD from competition while its growth levers begin to get pulled.

CMC, on the other hand, has a larger European presence and is focused on long steel products such as rebar and merchant bar. Both merchant bar and rebar are less exposed to import competition, and rebar is more regional in nature due to high shipping costs. Additionally, CMC’s European operations will not benefit from tariff implementation; in fact, they could be negatively impacted by retaliation from the EU, which was discussed recently following President Trump’s announcement that he would double steel tariffs from 25% to 50%.

Having owned STLD in the past, I took advantage of a big liberation day selloff and took a low-risk re-entry. Following the Monday rally after Trump’s latest tariff hike, I closed my position to lock in gains, having felt that the risk-reward was far less favorable and having grown concerned about the longer term risk of extremely high tariffs.

How Tariffs Might Boost CMC - Looking Back To 2018 Tariffs

The Section 232 steel tariffs that were enacted in March 2018 were fully reflected in CMC’s FY 2019 financials (ended 8/31/19). According to the U.S. International Trade Commission, the tariffs cut overall steel imports by about 24 %, leading to a roughly 2.4% increase in average domestic steel prices. U.S. steel production increased by roughly 1.9 % between 2018 and 2021. In the chart below, I have overlayed CMC’s North American Steel Products and Downstream Products average selling prices for FY2018 to FY2024 on top of CMC’s revenue and operating margin profile from FY 2015 to the TTM period.

CMC took advantage of the tariffs, capturing a 13% increase in North American Downstream products pricing and an 18% increase in Steel Products pricing in FY 2019. CMC also likely gained some organic market share due to tariffs. Overall tariffs likely played a supporting role in CMC’s operating margin climbing from 4.8% in FY 2018 to 5.8% in FY 2019. What complicates matters is that CMC was undergoing a company-wide transformation at the time, after the strategic acquisition of four of Gerdau’s EAF mini-mills and 33 fabrication shops, completed in November 2018. CMC also ramped up its second micro mill in Durant, OK during this time. So, capacity gains and what CMC considered to be synergies and logistical efficiency gains were also drivers of margin expansion.

The USITC report also found that downstream industries, such as appliances, auto parts, construction equipment, and fabricated steel, did not experience as significant a rise in prices as long steel and rebar, and there was a 0.6% decline in overall US downstream production.

Lower imports and higher prices may have boosted CMC‘s business, but the noted decline in downstream volume from the 2018 tariffs, though somewhat muted, must be accounted for, given the much higher tariff environment today. Will a 50% tariff on steel and aluminum, as opposed to the 2018 25% and 10% tariffs with some carve-outs, lead to worse end-product demand this time around?

Q2 Results Showed A Resilient Company

In Q2, CMC saw a YoY decline in total revenue of 5.1%. Scrap prices rose, and metal margin declined, with CMC’s management expressing confidence in being at or near the trough of metal margins. Operating margin was primarily impacted by the decrease in revenue and lower metal margin, as CMC effectively managed controllable costs. COGS declined 1.1% YoY during the quarter, while SG&A expense barely increased from the prior year period. The decline in operating margin flowed through to the bottom line, with diluted EPS falling from $0.73 per share in Q2 2024 to just $0.22 per share in Q2 2025.

The balance sheet remains strong, with cash and equivalents of $758 million and long-term debt of $1.0 billion. With TTM Operating Cash Flow of $795 million, the debt is easily manageable. Inventory was down 15% YoY to $978 million.

Capital expenditures have trended down in recent years ($607m in FY 2022, $367m TTM). The $32.4 million in OCF in Q2 included $67.5 million of cash outflows for working capital increases, mainly from increased scrap metal cost.

Long-Term Tailwinds and Near-Term Uncertainty Dominated The Q2 Discussion

Management reported positive trends during Q2, with higher mill pricing noted. The increase in downstream steel prices supports management's confidence that CMC might have reached a trough in profitability. Construction activity remains resilient according to CMC and the Dodge Momentum Index (DMI), a key measure of planned construction projects sitting at all-time highs and looking strong across various markets. Management noted YoY growth in finished steel product shipments, and saw its second-highest volume of new project awards since FY 2022, with a 10% sequential growth in backlog, giving the company confidence that demand will improve further.

CMC expects a better Q3 financial outcome, higher margin over scrap, and improved adjusted EBITDA margin.

If the trough truly is in, CMC will prove to investors that its through-the-cycle margins are indeed structurally improved. This is something I called out in my last article as a key to any investment case in CMC.

Higher market share, improved cost efficiency, logistics, and scale have driven this structural margin improvement for the company. Q2 is further proof of this. Management said that its execution cadence of its TAG initiatives is delivering results, which appear to have come faster than expected, with $15 million in savings achieved YTD and an additional $25 million expected in H2.

The demand tailwinds discussed in my last article appear to have improved slightly quarter-over-quarter, with new discussions introducing other demand levers. CMC indicated it sees a favorable environment for LNG terminal projects and discussions about German defense industry spending increases as a potential boost to European steel prices. Projects like these support high rebar demand, and any improvement in European defense spending should lessen the impact of oversupply of steel imports that have hampered CMC’s Poland plant and European operations.

Preparing For Q3 Earnings

When CMC reports its Q3 earnings on June 23 rd, I will be looking for commentary from management pointing to continued demand improvements and backlog growth, whether or not there appears to have been a significant pull forward of demand prior to tariff implementations, and if will look to confirm that metal margin has improved and the company’s operating margin, free cash flow margin and adjusted EBITDA margin has improved sequentially.

Risks From Federal Spending Attacks And 50% Tariffs

In Q2, CMC said it still sees project planning improvements in infrastructure projects, such as highways, and that it expected to see project awards pick up in Q3 and Q4, but government spending has become a point of discussion in recent months and it’s unclear if any of these projects are going to get shelved for the time being or if they will move forward. Additionally, construction spending suddenly dropped recently, highlighting this risk.

In my last article on CMC, I highlighted how the BIL acted as a long-term tailwind for the company. However, since President Trump took office, not only has he targeted the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) and clean energy projects, but he has also targeted funds distributed or planned to be distributed as part of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (BIL). This might mute some of the growth levers that CMC has at its disposal, but certainly appears to be a key driver of uncertainty. It changes my outlook slightly from feeling reasonably confident in the company’s near-term and medium-term growth potential to a being a bit more uncertain and offsets some of the potential benefits if tariff impacts.

I’m not going to get political here, but I struggle to understand what the end goal is with whipsaw tariff changes, daily rhetoric, and changing statements by the President. The idea that tariffs are meant to bring infrastructure improvements and jobs back to the United States, while the President simultaneously unwinds infrastructure investments made by his predecessor and Congress, perplexes me and adds to the uncertainty, impacting the investment case for CMC, as well as construction spending and planning.

While tariffs appear to be at least a short-term benefit to CMC, and could serve as a reason to believe the company can maintain market share gains over the medium to long term, as well as benefit from price increases, there is a risk that demand destruction could offset any tariff gains. A pullback in infrastructure investment and federal, state, and local government capital outlays due to pulled BIL, IRA, and other funding, and in private sector investments due to uncertainty in the market, will negatively impact CMC sales and profits.

I introduce these concerns not to saber rattle anyone politically, but to express my inability to wrap my mind around how this is likely to impact the steel market, the demand for finished steel products, and how it impacts CMC’s financials.

Whenever I can’t make sense of issues like this, I turn to technical analysis to help look for indications of a price inflection, rather than getting lost in a model or projections that cannot be made.

Technical Outlook

For long-term investing, I prefer to keep it simple with my technical analysis. For CMC, I will focus on using the 200-day moving average of the stock to look for potential low-risk buy points.

On a weekly chart, CMC is testing the 200-day MA. In the past, the 200 day has represented an area of resistance and support. I have labeled what looks to be bear market resistance on the RSI chart with a red line. In the past, CMC has rallied upon breaking this RSI resistance level, but the rallies have only been sustained with a volume increase and a break above this line. We do not have that volume increase or a breakout yet, but volume might be picking up as the stock nears both the 200-day and the bear market resistance line.

However, on an extended 2-day chart, CMC appears to show increasing volume while testing, peaking above, and falling back below the 200-day. The stock has peaked over the RSI bear market resistance line for now.

If CMC were to definitively break above the 200-day moving average of about $51.00 per share on increasing volume and rising RSI, I might get interested in opening a position if the fundamentals appear improved. For extra risk management, I might wait for a retest of the 200-day to ensure that a bullish trend is forming, rather than a false breakout. Perhaps we are getting that right now, perhaps it will fail and fall back below on price and sentiment.

Valuation

CMC’s earnings are depressed from a potential trough in both metal margin and revenue, so I recommend investors disregard trailing GAAP earnings for valuation, unless making adjustments, and focus on forward measures or adjusted measures to gain a better picture. I don’t think steel stocks that might be near a trough in earnings are easy to value using multiples, but here is Seeking Alpha’s Valuation chart showing a very mixed and unclear bag of metrics. Cyclical, commodity stocks are often better buys when they look expensive on the surface due to under-earning, which is why I prefer to look for inflection points and lean on technical analysis for confidence that a price move might be a legitimate re-rating by the market.

Conclusion

CMC appears to be a potential long-term investment that could yield solid returns due to its long-term growth drivers, including infrastructure development, investments in the electrical grid, LNG terminal projects, and data center expansions. Anytime a steel stock could be near an inflection in earnings, I get interested, but so far, there is too much uncertainty surrounding the policies of the current U.S. administration for me to jump in. I will be watching the stock closely and looking for a breakout on volume to confirm that the market is ready to buy into a turnaround in CMC’s outlook or potentially do some bottom fishing if the price falls deeper while fundamental performance improves.

For investors more confident in the outlook for construction and infrastructure spending, CMC may represent a good opportunity at this point. For now, I rate CMC a hold, but will be looking for an entry point in the near future.

data.title: Telephone and Data Systems: Shaky Present Amid Promising Fiber Bet - Hold
data.description:
Setting The Stage: Putting Recent Stock Gain & Key Developments In Context

Telephone and Data Systems, Inc. (TDS) is a US-based telecommunication company founded in 1968 and headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. It offers wireless broadband, TV and voice services to approximately 6 million customers. Barely a year, it announced its transformation, shifting its focus to fiber broadband.

Over the last year, the stock has soared more than 47%, beating the S&P 500's modest 12.8% gain. The surge in stock price has occurred despite a consistent downward momentum in revenue growth which has seen a growth of 1.99% in 2021 weaken each year to negative 3.80% in 2024 FY and negative 5.14% TTM.

With its shift from wireless to infrastructure business model, with $375-$425 million in CapEx for fiber expansion this year, is this stock an investor’s dream or rather a risky venture in the telecom industry?

The company made headlines with the sale of its wireless business, including the most cellular operations in the US to T-Mobile for about $4.4 billion. The deal is expected to close this year. The move and its active investment in fiber is a signal of a bold transition towards wireline broadband services. Nevertheless, macroeconomic challenges such as high-interest rates and intense competition from giants like AT&T and Verizon are a concern to TDS’s ability to effectively execute and sustain its transformation. In that effect, the most obvious question is, can this company leverage its restructuring to unlock value as the telecom industry evolves?

Breaking The Mold: How Restructuring Shakes The Status Quo

1. The Transition To Fiber

It’s important to note that this company’s core operations lie in its US cellular and the TDS Telecom segments. In the recent past, the company has adopted several restructuring moves in different spheres of its business. First off, the company is reviewing its US cellular business by exploring different measures, including divestiture of its wireless business. With the estimated $4.4 billion in the deal with T-Mobile, which includes a mix of cash and up to $2 billion in assumed debt, I see this as a great deal to optimize its capital structure and unlock cash for reinvestment. By retaining approximately 70% of its spectrum assets and its tower infrastructure, TDS has an opportunity for additional monetization if need be.

Wait a bit; is TDS justified in divesting its wireless business? Looking at its postpaid and prepaid operating metrics, I think it is worth the move. The former has seen consistent negative net additions in its handsets and negligible net additions in its connected devices.

Things are not very different in its prepaid metrics whose main highlight is a surge in its churn rate to its highest level of 4.17% in a year and an overall weakness in net additions.

To me, this is an indication of a saturated market or a weakening model which warrants significant overhaul, which the company is doing.

Alongside the divestiture, the company is expanding its fiber business. As of Q1 2025, its fiber-to-the-home network had reached about 52% of its service addresses, which is about 1.8 million marketable fiber services addresses.

This is a significant milestone and a move towards the right direction, as it aligns with the broader industry transition towards high-speed broadband. More importantly, the US Fiber market is estimated to grow to $16.47 billion by 2029, almost double its value in 2023. This offers a remarkable growth opportunity for TDS to exploit.

2. Why Am I viewing Fiber As A Growth Driver?

The telecom industry is shifting drastically towards fiber based broadband due to its reliability and high speed. As such, TDS current transition to this niche, with heavy investment such as the $375-$425 million in CapEx this year, underscores its adaptability to changing market dynamics.

Given its focus on fiber with almost 2 million connections and up to 8Gbps speed, TDS positions itself to capture a significant share in rural and suburban markets. Compared to the weak growth in operational metrics (mostly negative values) in the wireless business as demonstrated earlier, the new venture is seeing solid growth reporting thousands in net additions. For this reason, I am confident that this could be a long-term revenue growth driver as broadband demand surges.

3. The Downside: Risks

These opportunities are not without risks that limit value creation. Firstly, this industry is very competitive, with larger players such as AT&T and Verizon being the bigger fish than TDS. I think the company’s weakening revenue performance is an indication of the saturation in the wireless market. In its new venture, I think TDS's smaller scale given its lowest market cap of the three companies limits its pricing power and market reach.

However, I believe its capital recycling through selling its wireless assets while investing in fiber offers flexibility to compete and adapt. Additionally, its focus on small, mid-sized urban, suburban and rural markets serves as its niche which could boost its competitiveness.

Secondly, I believe this company faces execution risks. To successfully execute asset sales and fiber expansion requires precise management. In my view, any missteps in divestitures or delays in fiber rollout are likely to erode value. Further, it is still uncertain how emerging technologies like 6G and generative AI would impact telecom infrastructure, adding complexity to TDS’s long-term strategy.

Underwhelming Q1 2025: A Reason To Hold Despite The Fiber Investment

In Q1 2025, TDS reported a revenue of $1.15 billion, down 8.56% YoY missing estimates by more than $25 million. This was primarily due to divestitures of assets. However, over the last five years, the company’s revenue growth has been discouraging. Most recently, the company consistently reported negative growth figures, signaling its weakening business model.

In terms of profitability, net loss came in at $10 million translating to an EPS of -$0.09 which missed estimates by $0.08. This was a significant underperformance compared to a net income of $12 million in Q1 2024. Its consistent negative EPS since Q2 2024 is a testament to its operational challenges and the potential impacts of its divestitures.

From this performance, I come to my critical perspective of this analysis. Although the transition from wireless business seems to be a necessary transformation, the financials reveal the company is in a precarious short-term position. In my view, revenue decline and its net losses are an indication of a business at the crossroads of declining legacy operation and slow ramp-up of fiber. As such, I believe we need to exercise patience before we invest here until its current transformation leads to consistent improvement in both top and bottom lines.

Valuation

I am valuing this stock based on comparative PS ratio because its struggles with profitability make PE inappropriate. Compared to its peers VEON and USM, TDS has the lowest PS ratio at 0.82x, an indication that it is undervalued.

While some investors may view this as a buying opportunity, I am of a different opinion. I believe its lower PS is an indication of the market's concern about its growth prospects. Over the last one year, TDS revenue has plummeted by 19.33% compared to its peers’ double-digit revenue growth. In my view, I believe its underwhelming growth justifies its undervaluation and this doesn’t offer a buying opportunity unless its current transition offers tangible positive growth momentum.

Looking at the estimated revenue for the coming years, TDS is likely to wait for its new venture to turn around, its current financial woes, warranting a hold decision. This is based on its projected weak revenue growth through 2027. However, should its fiber expansion plan accelerate and growth momentum heats up, I would reassess the opportunity.

Conclusion

I believe TDS's shift from wireless to fiber broadband is warranted given the dismal performance of the former. The latter is showing signs of hope given its thousands in new net gains, and the market opportunity looks enormous. However, the company’s current financial performance necessitates patience until its transition restores consistent revenue growth and profitability.

data.title: Fresenius: Healthy Portfolio Add
data.description:
International stocks should be a core component of any healthy portfolio. International stocks offer diversification benefits, and often opportunities to invest capital at better valuations than are currently available in the U.S.

Furthermore, the policies of the Trump administration seem likely to reverse the historical strength of the USD.

Healthcare is an industry that will always be in demand, and, as medical science expands, and life expectancy gets longer, exposure to the sector seems to be a 'no-brainer'. The sector has been under pressure recently, as the Trump administration has taken aim at reducing drug pricing, and healthcare costs in general, and is seeking to implement reforms in several areas. Read this excellent PWC analysis which covers the topic in detail.

This uncertain landscape makes the case for gaining healthcare exposure outside the U.S. even more compelling.

Fresenius (FSNUY) (FSNUF) is a global healthcare company based in Germany, and listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange under the ticker FRE, it trades in the U.S. as FSNUY, FSNUF. FRE is an excellent choice to gain exposure to an international healthcare stock. Fresenius is one of Germany's largest companies by market cap, with around 176,000 employees earning EUR 21.5 billion in annual revenue. Fresenius operates as a leader in chronically ill patients, with Dialysis as a core competence. With a focus on providing lifetime care in a sector with growing demand, Fresenius is positioned well in a growing sector.

Furthermore, management has embarked on a clear strategic path to continued earnings growth, and is delivering strongly against its goals. Recent performance has been strong, and management is guiding for continued earnings growth.

The valuation is reasonable, at 13x forward earnings, and just above 1.1x book value.

The Segment

Fresenius is positioned across its product palette to serve the health needs of an ageing population. The chart below from Visual Capitalist gives a long-term projection. Key data points - between 2022 and 2050, the number of people over 65 years globally is set to grow by 60%, to 1 in 6 people. Developed countries, such as the U.S., Europe, Japan, as well as China, are at the top of the age range.

According to the Urban Institute, by 2040 one in 5 Americans will be over 65, compared to just one in 8 in 2020.

This translates into a huge demand for ongoing chronic illness and patient care needs. While there are plenty of over 65's without chronic illnesses, the healthcare needs of senior citizens are significantly higher.

Fresenius translates this into an expected 84% of deaths by 2030 to result from chronic diseases. Better medical care, and people living longer with chronic diseases is expected to create an unmet demand for health services.

Naturally, healthcare spending is expected to rise to take up 10% of global GDP already by 2030, with a trajectory that can only rise from there.

Fresenius has exposure to a broad range of products and services that serve this demographic, from IV fluids and drugs, to medicines, medical devices, and patient care facilities. It's like a one-stop shop for the aged.

The Company

Fresenius operates through two main business segments, Fresenius Kabi and Fresenius Helios. It owns investments in other healthcare businesses, most notably Fresenius Medical Care.

Fresenius Kabi specializes in intravenously administered generic drugs (IV drugs), clinical nutrition and infusion therapies. It also supplies hospitals with medical devices and products of transfusion technology. Kabi is the product production arm of the business, with a strong focus on transfusion, biosimilar, and nutrition products for chronically ill patients. This segment is the fastest growing, especially the biosimilar area.

Fresenius Helios is a private hospital operator, with over 130 facilities in Germany, including maximum care hospitals and rehabilitation centers. In Spain, under the brand 'Quironsalud', it has over 80 hospitals and outpatient centers, and 300 Occupational Risk Prevention Centers. On a combined basis, Helios is the largest private hospital operator in Europe.

Consolidated EBIT is currently roughly 50/50 between Kabi, and Helios, however, with a stronger growth in the Kabi segment, the mix is expected to shift in the future. This, and the disposal of Vamed, a service provider which was dragging on performance, is at the core of the business strategy.

Fresenius Medical Care runs a global network of dialysis clinics, providing treatment for patients with chronic kidney failures. This is the foundation business of Fresenius, however, FRE is now a minority shareholder, with a 32% share of float.

The Strategy

Group Strategy, which has been running since 2022, is divided into four phases. 2022 was tagged 'Reset' to bring the business through the difficult Covid period. 'Revitalize' followed, with a push to simplifying and focusing the firm. This phase involved divestment of non-core areas, and the separation into the two distinct segments, which I think of as 'Products' and 'Services'.

In 2025 we have entered the 'Rejuvenate' phase, which I see as all about executing strategy, investing in growth, and delivering on targets.

From here on, we see the rubber hit the road, and shareholder value really materialise.

Fresenius Kabi, the product business, has developed a ten-year strategic plan to better capitalise on the strong growth opportunities in play.

The focus will continue to be on products and services for critically and chronically ill patients, however investing in the faster growing segments while improving efficiency in the scaled areas.

Fresenius Kabi has defined three growth focus areas, and growing efficiency in the high volume, but lower margin IV business (3+1 strategy).

The growth levers are:

  • Broadening the biopharmaceutical offering, the smallest segment at just EUR 600m revenues, where biosimilars offer strong growth.
  • Further expansion of clinical nutrition - which has scaled rapidly into the U.S. to be the market leader in IV nutrition.
  • Growing the MedTech area - spearheaded by the Ivenix pump, a state-of-the-art infusion device launching Fresenius into the U.S.

The growth levers are:

  • Broadening the biopharmaceutical offering, the smallest segment at just EUR 600m revenues, where biosimilars offer strong growth.
  • Further expansion of clinical nutrition - which has scaled rapidly into the U.S. to be the market leader in IV nutrition.
  • Growing the MedTech area - spearheaded by the Ivenix pump, a state-of-the-art infusion device launching Fresenius into the U.S.

Spotlight on Biosimilars

Even though Fresenius targets growth in three of the Kabi segments, it's worth spending some time on the smallest segment, Biosimilars. These offer the clearest and most ambitious growth opportunity.

Biosimilars are the biopharma equivalent of generic drugs. Simply put, biosimilars are made from living cells, not chemicals. They give an opportunity to reduce the cost of care once patents expire. As such, biosimilars are positioned to capitalise on the drive to reduce medical care costs, a key threat perceived in the pharma space.

Biosimilars are used to provide lifelong treatment of chronic conditions such as bowel, kidney, arthritis, cancer and diabetes.

Growth in biosimilar demand for the next decade is projected to be close to 20% CAGR. According to this report by Claight, in the U.S. alone, revenues are expected to grow from $24bn today to $124bn by 2034.

Fresenius has a strong pipeline to grow into this recurring revenue marketplace.

If Fresenius can secure growth at the current market share, the biosimilar business would grow from the current EUR 600m today to EUR 2.6bn by 2034.

However, as this business is in the early stages, revenue growth is scaling at much higher levels. Q1 2025 saw a 40% year-on-year growth in the biopharma segment.

What's really impressive is the clarity of the pipeline. A key strength of the biosimilar business model, is there is a multi-year target for the patent expiry window to open. This enables a targeted development pipeline to match patent expiry delivery dates.

Fresenius Helios is already the leading private healthcare service provider in Europe, and seeks to secure that further. This is a much more 'steady as she goes' strategy. Running hospitals is a capital intensive business, and Fresenius is already the market leader in its two markets. Growth in revenue is likely to match GDP growth, and earnings growth above that will come through operational improvements.

Helios Germany is in a mature phase, with slow growth and a tight EBIT margin driven by nursing care costs. The name of the game here is efficiency via driving cross-sector healthcare and developing specialised hospitals which operate in a regional network to offer best in class patient care.

Secondly, they seek to develop closer synergies between their outpatient clinics and hospital network.

Spain offers a faster growth in demand for hospital and other healthcare services. Margins are higher. They also have a higher growth market play with a foray into Lat-Am. Here the theme of better organisation, specialisation and integration is coupled with plans to expand hospital capacity, and 'feeder' outpatient clinics.

Targets

Fresenius is not overly specific about targets, and in my view, the outlook seems conservative. At a mid-point, 5% revenue growth in Helios, with improving margins, 3% earnings growth in the IV business, 5.5% in the nutrition segment, 9% revenue growth and margin improvement in the Med-tech segment, and significant growth in the Biopharma segment. The latter in the 'mid-term' is looking at EUR 1bn in revenues up from the existing EUR 600m.

Business Performance

The Q1 2025 performance is a big jump towards these targets, with a 12% growth in year-on-year earnings per share.

Revenues were up 7%, and all segments reported results that exceeded targets. The leverage ratio of 3x is at the top of the management target range of 2.5 - 3 x.

Kabi

Kabi reported a strong growth in EBIT margins, from 15.1% to 16.8% year-on-year. Quarter on quarter revenues were flat, which looks a bit soft compared to Q4.

Helios

Helios, on the other hand, saw better than expected revenue growth year-on-year and quarter-on-quarter, with declining margins. Under the hood, this splits into strong growth and margins in Spain, with Germany experiencing an expected margin drop due to the removal of some short-term subsidies.

At the group level, an important return to positive cash flow of EUR 2bn despite a EUR 900m in Capex was great to see. Netting off the one-off divestment of FME, the group is still throwing off about EUR1.5bn in cash on around EUR 5.5bn in revenues.

Valuation

Having been in the doldrums through the post Covid recovery, Fresenius shares have caught a bid in the last 12 months, and have increased nearly 60% in the last 12 months, eclipsing the S&P 500 (SPY). Much of the growth has come since the good Q1 results. Part of the uptick mirrors the general performance of the German market (DAX).

This market bid is clearly driven by the turnaround performance, with the trailing price-to-earnings at nearly 21x, while the forward PE offers a more compelling valuation at 13x.

Competitors

Given the broad business mix in different segments, I found it hard to find an appropriate competitor to benchmark Fresenius against. The company has components of med-tech, healthcare providers, and pharma. I find it more appropriate to consider Fresenius on its own merits rather than trying to benchmark.

My own discounted cash flow valuation using an assumption of 5% annualised revenue growth and a margin improvement generating 9% EPS growth results in a EUR 51 fair value, a 16% upside.

This is only slightly more optimistic than the Wall St analyst reported on Seeking Alpha. However, it looks as though the analyst is not active, with no recommendation change recorded since 2023.

Morningstar has a more up-to-date rating, incorporating the Q1 results. This has a four-star buy rating, with a EUR 53 price target, around 20% above today's price.

All in all, I see my DCF fair value of EUR 51 as reasonably conservative.

Risks To Fresenius

Healthcare reforms in the U.S.

I see this as a low risk to Fresenius. Currently, over 70% of its income is earned in Europe. The US offers growth in Biosimilars, which I see as having potential tailwinds from US healthcare cost initiatives.

GLP-1 reducing patient demand

If GLP has a dramatic impact on diabetes incidence, in the long term, this could reduce the demand for dialysis solutions. In reality, this seems a long way off even in the U.S. and diabetes risks globally are increasing. Furthermore, Fresenius has a broad income base, of which dialysis is only one part.

Margin pressure in Hospitals segment

This is a key risk for Fresenius which has already experienced challenges here in 2021/2022 when fixed costs remained high while demand dropped. There is also the risk of nursing staff costs rising faster than billings.

Regulatory risk

As a healthcare provider, Fresenius faces regulatory scrutiny in several areas, from patient care to drug protocols. Keeping ahead of regulatory demands is a major risk. I feel confident that Fresenius is well-placed to manage this, as health code compliance is its core business.

Conclusion

Fresenius is a unique company which straddles a range of products and services in the chronic care segment. This is a segment with an almost guaranteed long-term pathway of demand.

Fresenius has emerged from challenging times with a business that is positioned in critical segments. While its targets are quite modest, business performance looks set to exceed these.

Many of the risks faced by the healthcare segment are relatively low for Fresenius, especially in its growth areas.

A fair value estimate using conservative assumptions shows Fresenius as undervalued, even after a 60% 12 month price uplift.

I see Fresenius as a buy at current levels, as a long-term hold for both international and healthcare exposure.

data.title: Unseen Upside: The Commercial Rebound Nobody Called
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy | Art Jones, Senior Director, Global Real Estate Research and Strategy | Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist | Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

Changes in both commercial real estate (CRE) total returns and housing prices have historically moved in tandem. However, they have diverged from each other over the past couple of years, driven by the higher interest rate environment seen since the end of the pandemic, which, counterintuitively, has lifted housing prices while simultaneously weighing on CRE values.

Looking ahead, we expect the current divergence between residential housing and commercial real estate to normalize over the near term. Home price appreciation is likely to slow amid an increasingly regionally bifurcated housing market, while CRE total returns are expected to continue accelerating — driven primarily by income, as capital returns remain muted. We believe investors should prepare to accept that the outperformance of CRE versus housing will hold for the foreseeable future, and may even diverge again.

CRE and housing prices experience a rare divergence

Year-over-year (YoY) changes in unlevered commercial real estate total returns and housing prices have diverged since Q1 2023. This is because quarterly total returns for CRE declined from 4Q22 to 3Q24, according to the National Council of Real Estate Investment Fiduciaries (NCREIF). At the same time, home prices rose over the same period, as measured through the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller U.S. National Home Price NSA Index. Most recently, however, the relationship has begun to flip, with year-over-year changes in CRE total returns recently changing from negative to positive in 4Q24 and continuing to accelerate in 1Q25, while housing prices have decelerated.

Home prices and commercial real estate total returns

YoY change, 1988–present

Divergences between CRE and housing have been rare in history, which — including this instance — have only occurred 20% of the time since the late 1980s. The divergences have largely been a byproduct of the lead-lag relationship heading into and throughout recoveries of economic downturns. In most instances, housing downturns are a leading indicator for CRE. Below are historical instances of this dynamic.

  • the seven quarters from 3Q10 to 1Q12 (when CRE rebounded first, post GFC);
  • the seven quarters from 1Q07 to 3Q08 (when housing prices fell before CRE before the GFC);
  • the seven quarters from 1Q92 to 3Q92 (when CRE prices fell post S&L crisis while housing recovered), and;
  • the two quarters in 4Q90/1Q91 (when housing prices fell before CRE during the recession).

What makes today’s episode even more rare is that it has occurred despite an economic downturn. Indeed, this is the first time where CRE prices have turned negative, absent a recession and a corresponding decline in housing prices.

Disparate reaction to rising interest rates

The recent decline in CRE valuations, which saw unlevered prices decline by nearly 20% and levered prices fall by more than 30%, was driven by a combination of rising interest rates, tightening lending conditions, and decelerating net operating income growth. This came despite fundamentals remaining solid, with net operating income growth averaging +5% in 2023 across property types and +3.2% in 2024 versus the historical average of +2.5%.

By comparison, housing prices have risen to new record levels despite mortgage rates increasing at the fastest pace in at least 40 years to levels the market hasn’t seen since 2000, according to Morgan Stanley Research. However, many homeowners are locked into low mortgage rates, leaving them little incentive to sell their homes. The combination of higher home prices and higher interest rates has pushed home affordability to challenging levels, creating a very constrained supply environment, with listing volumes as a share of outstanding stock of single-family ownership at its lowest levels in the data series' 40-year history.1

The times they are a-changin’

Going forward, we anticipate an environment that supports CRE returns surpassing those of housing prices. In fact, interest rates drifting lower as economic activity slows may support CRE valuations. This could counterintuitively weigh on house prices as housing affordability improves, leading to a thaw in frozen supply. In other words, it’s an unwinding of the disconnected backdrop over the past couple of years, and it’s not unreasonable to expect the opposite to play out in the coming years.

There are early signs that this is already starting to occur as home prices have decelerated year-over-year to +3.4% in 1Q25 compared to a peak of +21% in 1Q22 and 6.6% as recently as 1Q24, driven by the emergence of a bifurcated market for home price appreciation.

While for-sale housing inventory remains very tight nationally, there are differences across the nation. Certain markets in Florida and Texas, for example, are seeing rising inventory compared to 4Q19 levels, while many markets in the Northeast and the Midwest are still down significantly.1

This is leading to pronounced geographic differences in home prices. For instance, based on an analysis of 894 markets tracked by the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI), 30 out of the top 50 markets with the lowest home price appreciation in 1Q25 were in Florida or Texas. Meanwhile, 30 out of the top 50 markets with the highest home price appreciation were in Indiana, Kentucky, Ohio, and Wisconsin; only two markets were in the Sunbelt states.

Annual home price appreciation by state

Year-over-year changes in home prices, 1Q24 vs. 1Q25

By comparison, year-over-year changes in CRE total returns turned positive in 4Q24 and continued to accelerate higher in 1Q25 to +2.8%, given a combination of still solid fundamentals, improving lending conditions, and interest rates that have declined from peaks in late 2023. Our full-year 2025 projections for CRE are +5% as income returns are offsetting muted capital returns. This would mark the first time since 4Q22 that CRE returns are higher than housing prices. We expect annualized total returns to increase toward +7% over the next 5 years as capital returns turn positive coupled with solid income returns. We believe that seeing annualized 10-year returns of 8-9% that are in line with historical rolling average 10-year annualized returns is not unreasonable.

Average rolling annual 10-year return

At various start dates

By decade

Source: NCREIF, Principal Real Estate. Data as of March 31, 2025.

Investment implications

These disparate outcomes should remind investors to be increasingly aware of idiosyncratic forces that are driving valuations within single family and commercial real estate segments. Looking ahead, we expect year-over-year CRE total returns to outpace housing price growth, in line with their historical relationship — and potentially surpass the long-term average spread of +300 basis points — as CRE momentum builds and housing slows.

Ultimately, the traditional role of interest rates in real estate valuations has become less prevalent in the post-pandemic period, but interest rates are not the sole driver of CRE valuations. We believe income, which can vary by property type, will also be an important driver of CRE total returns over the next cycle. A cycle that is more likely to resemble the environment that persisted in the decades prior to the Global Financial Crisis versus the somewhat financially engineered returns post-GFC, that were driven by historically low interest rates.

Footnote

[1] Morgan Stanley Research. Global Securitized Products Mid-Year Outlook: Good Things Come to Those Who Wait.

data.title: TFI International: Cross-Border Presence And Robust Cash Flow Justify Undervaluation
data.description:
For more than four months since my previous coverage, TFI International, Inc. (NYSE:TFII) has continued to face softer market conditions. Yet, it also maintained its resilience and liquidity with its positive earnings and robust cash flow. Considering its current valuation, TFII appears more fairly valued, even at a discount today. Bullish signals are still weak. But buying opportunities for this stock are present.

TFII Q1 2025 Recap: Challenged, Not Overwhelmed

TFI International, Inc. continued to experience weaker freight demand and stubborn inflation. The lower volume of goods transported and weaker consumption due to macroeconomic uncertainty remain a challenge. With new tariffs on the horizon, businesses may have already become more cautious about their production and pricing. These things continue to affect the performance of companies in the trucking industry.

TFI reflected all this, especially in its LTL segment. Its revenue without fuel surcharge amounted to $679M, down by -13.3% YoY from $783.5M. Pricing flexibility remained a challenge in this segment as volume weakened further. In its operations in the US, shipments dropped from 1.4M to 1.3M, while revenue per shipment excluding fuel fell from $349.43 to $335.19. Its logistics segment also remained weak, as the segment revenue decreased by -12.8% YoY.

On a lighter note, its Truckload segment remained its cornerstone after completely offsetting the weakness in the other two segments. Its revenue was $662.9M, up by 66.7% YoY from $397.7M. Its continued expansion in this segment drove this uptrend. Note that it acquired two businesses during the quarter. This was visible in its specialized TL segment. Its revenue per week without fuel increased from $4,187 to $4,201. This shows that its expansion in this business was fruitful. As a result, the total operating revenue of all its business segments amounted to $1.71B, a 6.2% increase versus $1.61B YoY. If we include fuel surcharges, the total revenue will still be higher at $1.96B versus $1.87B YoY.

With respect to its costs and expenses, TFII remained prudent with its asset management. It also benefited from the continued oil price decrease. Fuel surcharges dropped, and so did fuel expenses. If we deduct the operating income from the revenue, we will see notable expense decreases in LTL and Logistics. Although they were not enough to compensate for the lower revenue, they still helped ensure a positive operating income.

Tariffs Versus Oil Prices

The implementation of new tariffs will be unfavorable for freight and logistics companies, including TFII. One simple reason is that tariffs will increase the cost incurred by exporters to the US. On the other hand, US consumers may face higher prices since tariffs are inflationary in nature. This may lower freight demand in the long run. Considering this, policymakers must be careful with the implementation of new policies. The thing is, inflation is still a positive number, so prices are still increasing, although at a slower rate. Thankfully, the Fed remains on guard with its policy cycle. It keeps the interest rate untouched as it anticipates the impact of new tariffs. This is a prudent move to stabilize inflation.

On a lighter note, the decreasing oil prices may help partially offset tariffs. Currently, the price of WTI per barrel is 25% lower than the 52-week-high. With the planned OPEC oil hike this July, global oil prices may stay at the current price range. If this trend persists, fuel expenses may also decrease. This will benefit various industries, such as shipping, logistics, airlines, manufacturing, and food. Transporting goods will be less expensive, which can help cushion the blow of tariffs.

Potential Mitigants And Drivers

Amidst these challenges, I believe TFII still has some advantages to help it navigate an uncertain environment. One factor to consider is its cross-border infrastructure. Having extensive operations in the US and Canada, coupled with its established networks, TFII has some flexibility in shifting trade routing and freight allocation. This will be its edge over its US-only or Canada-only peers.

Moreover, its strategic acquisitions allow it to diversify its service portfolio and balance its asset-heavy and asset-light services. This business mix will help it reprice contracts or switch transport modes. For example, it can switch from TL to intermodal, and vice versa, depending on new trade costs. It will also help it absorb higher tariff costs more flexibly relative to its asset-heavy-only peers. After all, it has already increased its TL segment presence in the US. On top of that, its expansion in asset-light logistics in the US will help it pivot or shift to domestic US freight or third-party services if tariffs raise cross-border hauls substantially. This flexibility is in line with its cost and expense management to ensure positive income and robust cash flow.

What’s more interesting in TFII is its strong liquidity, even after its recent acquisitions. Its high cash inflows show its expanding operations generate high returns despite market headwinds. For instance, its FCF of $160M last Q1 means that TFI converted 9.3% of its revenue into free cash. This is higher than in Q1 2024 with an FCF/Sales conversion of 7.6%.

Moreover, borrowings have increased in the past five years. But we can attribute it to its continued expansion through acquisitions. With TFII’s current liquidity and performance, repayments can be sustained. Excluding lease-related liabilities, it has a decent Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.2x. This means that TFII only needs two years to repay all borrowings at once, even if it doesn’t have to. It is also worth noting that most of its borrowings have longer maturity. For instance, only 9.0% of unsecured notes of $1.66B will mature within three years. So, TFII still has enough time and capital buffer to prepare for repayments.

Valuation

TFII has plunged by -36% since my previous coverage, despite my buy rating. One thing I’ve learned is that price ratios are a crucial tool to assess a stock, even if they are historical values. If you can remember, both the P/E and P/B suggested overpricing before. But now, things have changed quite a bit.

At its price, TFII trades at about 19x its EPS, which is expensive relative to the five-year average of 16x. If we use this to derive its fair value, this will give a TP of $73.77 and a downside risk of 14%. But if we use the P/B, it will suggest a TP of $108.58 and a 25% upside potential. While it also justifies the recent correction, it shows that the rate of decrease has become a bit too stretched. Either way, both ratios show that the current price appears more realistic and buyable than before.

To test the price behavior, I also did a regression analysis with 25 time series. Note that I used semi-annual values, so I did first-order differencing to at least minimize the impact of seasonality. The correlation of 98% shows that when EPS and/or BVPS decrease, the price will most likely follow them. What’s more important is their p-value, which shows that BVPS is a more reliable measure. This shows that only 0.028 or 2.8% of the data happened by chance, which removes the randomness of their relationship. This is contrary to the EPS, which shows high variability with 30% randomness.

/Analysis

TFII’s momentum is still weak. But we can note some improvements after the price correction. The price is slightly increasing and moving above the 50-day SMA. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has already flattened compared to previous months, with a sharp downward trend. This shows a shift in market sentiments and a lower selling pressure after reaching the bottom. Yet, it is important to note that both are still below the 200-day SMA. Once the price crosses above it, bullish signals will become stronger. It will be better if the 50-day SMA also surpasses it.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI are consistent with the price trend. At this point, we cannot detect any bullish or bearish divergence yet. Nonetheless, there is enough room for a buy position as the RSI stays below the overbought region.

What’s interesting is how the price has been behaving to the Fibonacci Retracement recently. The price touched the 0.236 retracement point last May 13-15 before weakening a bit. Currently, the stock appears to be consolidating as it tests its first resistance at $91-93. With that in mind, there’s a chance for the price to regain its momentum and reach a higher point of 0.382 at $104.73. Hence, the stock is already at a bargain.

Conclusion

TFI International, Inc. faces some market challenges today. But its profitability, robust cash flow, and cross-border presence can help it lower risks and even use them to its advantage. In addition, it is already fairly valued with a decent upside. Technicals are still recovering but show new buying opportunities at the current price. I’m reiterating my rating of buy on this stock.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Uncertainty Squared
data.description:
When you thought things couldn't possibly get any weirder… ("Have a nice day, DJT!")

"Uncertainty" is the word of the year, for an environment dominating by a unique array of uncertainties - policy, market, economic, geopolitical… There are tariff and trade war uncertainties, highlighted by the President's uniquely whimsical approach. With a third of the 90-day pause period remaining, it's unclear how many trade deals can be squeezed into such a short negotiations window - and how much hardball the administration is willing to play as the deadline approaches. More specifically, complex trade agreements with the EU and China will require extensive, and likely protracted, talks.

While markets are said to abhor uncertainty, stocks these days seem to eat it up. It's basically uncertainty on top of uncertainty. The world has never been so awash in speculative finance, ensuring aberrant market behavior. Never has the global leveraged speculating community been as colossal and powerful. Egregious Treasury "basis trade" leveraging drives unprecedented overall hedge fund leverage. Household (loving dip buying) market participation is unparalleled, with the proliferation of online accounts, options trading, and herd-like speculation creating extraordinary market-moving power.

Importantly, the current remarkable backdrop creates a uniquely potent "risk on/risk off" market dynamic. Case in point: From April 25th '24 lows to July 10th 24' highs, the Bloomberg MAG7 Index surged 38%. From July 10th highs to August 5th '24 lows, MAG7 dropped 22%. Then, from August 5th lows to December 18th highs, the index surged 51%. From December 18th highs to April 7th '25 lows, MAG7 sank 33%. And from April 7th lows to Friday's (June 6th) close, the index has rallied 35%.

One upshot is the incredible variability in perceived wealth (and "wealth effects") for an index with $14.7 TN of market capitalization - a welcome tailwind during "risk on" and unnerving headwinds when "risk off" strikes.

There are also consequential market liquidity impacts. For an index of companies at the epicenter of retail and institutional speculation, MAG7-related margin debt, options, and other related derivatives produce extraordinary liquidity effects (money market fund assets up another $67bn last week!). Rising stock prices stoke self-reinforcing liquidity creation, while self-offs heighten risks of illiquidity, deleveraging, and market dislocation. Loose conditions certainly reinforce the bullish notion of unlimited "money" available for massive AI investment requirements. These days, it's one extraordinarily fine line between boom and bust.

The Goldman Sachs Short Index surged 10.8% this week, having now rallied 40% off April 7th trading lows. The equities "squeeze" has corresponded with dramatically lower CDS prices, narrower Credit spreads, and sharply reduced risk premiums.

Unique uncertainty and myriad clear and present risks beckon for market risk hedging. But massive hedging and speculating only compound marketplace uncertainty and instability. As we witnessed in April and last August, "risk off" now tends to quickly spiral into deleveraging, illiquidity, and dislocation. Meanwhile, acute Bubble fragilities ensure swift policy responses (i.e., Trump's tariff pause) that trigger destabilizing unwinds of hedges and bearish bets - along with "buy the dip" FOMO madness. Orderly market adjustment no longer appears possible.

A speculative marketplace of such extremes creates an extraordinarily unstable liquidity backdrop. Risk embracement (with leverage and derivatives) promotes liquidity excess, while bouts of risk aversion trigger deleveraging and resulting tighter financial conditions. Loose financial conditions underpin economy activity, while "risk off" comes with a destabilizing tightening of corporate lending and debt issuance. April's major tightening saw a temporary halt to high-risk debt issuance (i.e., junk bonds and leveraged loans).

There are today two notably powerful countervailing forces. The "risk on" squeeze dynamic is spurring speculative leveraging and the perception of abundant liquidity. Meanwhile, global bond markets signal waning demand and fledgling liquidity issues. Strong risk market gains, the perception of liquidity abundance, and attendant complacency work to mask mounting risks to Treasury and global bond market (i.e., Japan and UK) stability.

June 4 - Bloomberg (Alice Gledhill and Ruth Carson): "A spate of poorly-received longer-dated sovereign bond auctions worldwide has raised questions about the willingness of investors to fund the spending plans of governments from the US to Japan. Japan's 30-year bond sale Thursday was the third in as many weeks to show signs of a cold shoulder from buyers, with one measure of demand the weakest since 2023. Attention now turns to US Treasury auctions of 10- and 30-year debt next week, a test of appetite after recent similar issuance from other sovereigns failed to ignite. Tuesday's auction of 12-year Australian government debt saw the weakest demand in about six years and Wednesday's post-election South Korean 30-year sale saw the lowest investor appetite since 2022."

June 5 - Financial Times (Ian Smith): "Auctions of government bonds are usually so routine that they generate little attention. But Japan's sale of 20-year debt last month was an exception. As financial newswires flashed the dismal results around the world, the prices of the longest dated Japanese sovereign bonds dropped sharply… An auction of US 20-year bonds the following day also attracted a lukewarm response. Close attention to the finer details of government bond auctions and higher yields on longer-dated debt are symptoms of the same thing: wobbling investor appetite for such instruments just at the moment when many finance ministries are planning record levels of issuance, and as the world economy enters a new and uncertain era. For the first time in almost a generation, governments are starting to face resistance from the market when they try to sell long-term debt. 'It's a classic supply-and-demand mismatch problem, but on a global scale,' says Amanda Stitt, a fixed-income specialist at… T Rowe Price. 'The era of cheap, long-term funding is over, and now governments are jostling in a crowded room of sellers.'"

More from Jamie Dimon…

June 2 - Reuters (Suzanne McGee and Nupur Anand): "JPMorgan… CEO Jamie Dimon said… the rising U.S. national debt is a 'big deal' that could create a 'tough time' for the bond market that causes spreads to widen, he told Fox Business... 'If people decide that the U.S. dollar isn't the place to be, you could see credit spreads gap out; that would be quite a problem… It hurts the people raising money. That includes small businesses, that includes loans to small businesses, includes high yield debt, includes leveraged lending, includes real estate loans. That's why you should worry about volatility in the bond market.'"

When Citadel is on edge…

June 5 - Reuters (Carolina Mandl and Davide Barbuscia): "Citadel's… CEO Ken Griffin said… it is 'unfathomable' that a financial instrument to protect against an eventual U.S. default is being priced at levels close to some European countries. 'I never thought in my life I would see the U.S. priced higher in risk cost than a number of countries like Spain, Germany or France,' he said… 'You gotta be kidding me.' Griffin said the credit default swap (CDS) market has some issues with liquidity which impact prices, but still he considered that conversations around how close the swaps are trading are 'unfathomable'… 'The United States' fiscal house is not in order. You cannot run deficits of six or 7% at full employment after years of growth. That's just fiscally irresponsible,' he said."

June 6 - Bloomberg (Katherine Doherty and Bernard Goyder): "Citadel Securities President Jim Esposito said the US deficit and mounting government debt levels are a 'ticking time bomb,' adding his voice to the chorus of financial executives soundings warnings about America's deteriorating fiscal outlook. Esposito… said how President Donald Trump's administration responds to the situation will be 'super important.' 'I do think the stock of debt and the budget deficit is a ticking time bomb," Esposito said… at a Piper Sandler conference. 'No one is smart enough to predict when exactly it will rear its ugly head.'"

When Goldman and BlackRock turn more cautious…

June 5 - Financial Times (Martin Arnold and Brooke Masters): "Goldman Sachs has reined in risk-taking due to market volatility triggered by Donald Trump's trade war and fears that rising US debt will erode investor appetite for dollar-denominated assets, a senior bank executive has said. John Waldron, president and chief operating officer…, told a Goldman podcast… the investment bank had 'moderated our risk positioning' since the US president announced an across-the-board tariff increase on its trading partners on April 2, adding, 'that's a sensible thing for us to do'. The reduction in risk-taking by one of the world's most influential financial institutions underlines how Wall Street traders have been unnerved by the shockwaves that ripped through markets after Trump unleashed his trade war."

June 5 - Financial Times (Peter Eavis): "BlackRock chief executive Larry Fink said the US was 'going to hit the wall' unless the economy grows quickly enough to manage higher deficits from government spending, as a growing chorus of financiers warn about the country's mounting debt. Fink… characterised the deficit as one of the 'two most consequential issues' US politicians are ignoring, as President Donald Trump looks to pass tax cuts that will add $2.4tn to the national debt over the next decade. 'We have a pending tax bill that's going to add $2.3tn, $2.4tn on the back of that,' Fink said, pointing to the $36tn in existing US debt. 'If we don't find a way to grow at 3% a year… we're going to hit the wall.' 'If we cannot unlock the growth and if we're going to continue to stumble along at a 2% economy, the deficits are going to overwhelm this country,' Fink said…"

Ten-year Treasury yields jumped 12 bps Friday to 4.51%, following stronger-than-expected May non-farm payrolls data. At 4.12%, five-year Treasury yields surged 16 bps this week to 4.12%, within five bps of the highest yield since February.

Until Friday, the Treasury market had for the most part disregarded rallying stocks and loosening conditions. The preponderance of recent data has indicated a weakening of economic momentum. ISM Manufacturing (48.5), factory orders, durable goods orders, vehicle sales and the ADP employment gain (lowest in two years) were all reported weaker-than-expected. ISM Services in May posted the weakest reading (49.9) since last June, with a notable drop in New Orders.

But Friday's stronger-than-expected jobs data struck a bond market nerve. Labor markets don't appear to be weakening sufficiently to restrain elevated wage growth - or to engender Fed concern. Average Hourly Earnings rose 0.4%, with y-o-y gains of 3.9%. This followed Tuesday's JOLTS data, which had job openings rising almost 200,000 in April to 7.391 million (almost 300k above forecast). While disappointing on job growth (37k), ADP's report confirmed sticky wage inflation ("stayers" 4.5% y-o-y, "changers" 7.0%). The ISM Services Employment component rose 1.7 points to an expanding (3-month high) 50.7.

Tariff price inflation is looming. Garnering less attention, dollar weakness is also conducive to rising import prices. Meanwhile, inflation fears have been held in check by expectations of a weakening economy. Fragile bond market sentiment has also been underpinned by the view that the full-employment mandate would become the Fed's primary focus. This support is in jeopardy.

The rapid return of market wealth effects and significant loosening of financial conditions increase the likelihood of near-term upside economic surprises. What's more, the administration's more aggressive approach with apprehensions and deportations has potential to reinforce labor market tightness and wage inflation.

And so much uncertainty with that "one big, beautiful bill"…

June 6 - Bloomberg (Erik Wasson): "Congressional Republicans are siding with Donald Trump in the messy divorce between the president and Elon Musk, an optimistic sign for eventual passage of a tax cut bill at the root of the two billionaires' public feud. Lawmakers are largely taking their cues from Trump and sticking by the $3 trillion bill at the center of the White House's economic agenda. Musk… has threatened to help primary anyone who votes for the legislation, but lawmakers are betting that staying in the president's good graces is the safer path to political survival. 'The tax bill is not in jeopardy. We are going to deliver on that,' House Speaker Mike Johnson told reporters... 'I'll tell you what - do not doubt, don't second guess and do not challenge the President of the United States Donald Trump,' he added. 'He is the leader of the party. He's the most consequential political figure of our time.'"

"The most consequential political figure" has his hands full.

June 6 - Bloomberg: "In the early hours of Wednesday, Donald Trump declared that Xi Jinping was 'VERY TOUGH, AND EXTREMELY HARD TO MAKE A DEAL WITH!!!' Some 36 hours later, the US leader said he got what he wanted: A commitment to restore the flow of rare earth magnets. It's less clear what Xi got in return, apart from putting a lid on further punitive US measures… 'This call provides tactical de-escalation for US-China relations,' said Sun Chenghao, a fellow at the Center for International Security and Strategy at Tsinghua University in Beijing. 'However, China's core demands - equal sanction relief, reciprocal enforcement mechanisms, and an end to tech containment - remain critical for sustainable agreements… Without substantive US adjustments in follow-up talks and policies, the consensus may not translate into long-term stability'."

At this point, there's ample confirmation that the Geneva trade agreement and "total reset" were less than advertised. The Chinese have not wavered, and it has become clear that they have executed a thoughtfully crafted strategy to use rare earth minerals and magnets for trade war leverage. President Trump has been fond of claiming that trade wars are "easy to win." He and his administration taunted the Chinese for having such a weak hand to play. But a country that processes 90% of elements critical for producing automobiles, aircraft, military armaments, high tech components, clean energy, and such - has cards strong enough to make competitors sweat.

June 6 - Reuters (Laurie Chen): "China has signalled for more than 15 years that it was looking to weaponise areas of the global supply chain, a strategy modelled on longstanding American export controls Beijing views as aimed at stalling its rise. The scramble in recent weeks to secure export licences for rare earths, capped by Thursday's telephone call between U.S. and Chinese leaders Donald Trump and Xi Jinping, shows China has devised a better, more precisely targeted weapon for trade war. Industry executives and analysts say while China is showing signs of approving more exports of the key elements, it will not dismantle its new system. Modelled on the United States' own, Beijing's export licence system gives it unprecedented insight into supplier chokepoints in areas ranging from motors for electric vehicles to flight-control systems for guided missiles. 'China originally took inspiration for these export control methods from the comprehensive U.S. sanctions regime,' said Zhu Junwei, a scholar at the Grandview Institution, a Beijing-based think tank…"

June 6 - New York Times (Daisuke Wakabayashi and Berry Wang): "During his phone call with President Trump, Xi Jinping leaned on a maritime analogy to try to salvage the fragile trade truce that seemed to be fracturing from a series of escalating punitive economic measures. The Chinese leader compared the relationship between the United States and China to a large ship, with the two men serving as powerful captains holding the rudder firmly to maintain the proper course. The analogy also came with a warning. Do not let others steer the ship off course and jeopardize the relationship… In a readout from the Chinese government, Mr. Xi emphasized on the 90-minute call that the two leaders needed to 'steer clear of various disturbances or even sabotage'… Yun Sun, director of the China program at the Stimson Center…, said China saw an opportunity to use 'top leader diplomacy' to send this message to Mr. Trump directly: 'Hold off your hawks. The responsibility is on the top leaders. If you want a good relationship, don't let your cabinet members or team run freely with their crazy ideas.'"

Thursday's Trump/Xi was a positive development. It makes sense that Beijing believed it was time for President Trump to hear China's perspective directly from the horse's mouth. Chinese officials have criticized the administration's "two-faced" approach. So, with President Trump so eager to chum around with Xi Jinping, why not use the opportunity to pry some distance between the President and his more hawkish advisors?

"If you want a good relationship, don't let your cabinet members or team run freely with their crazy ideas." What if "crazy" radiates from the top? The President and his administration are determined to thwart China's global superpower ambitions. They'll continue to approach this objective on multiple fronts (tariffs, export controls, sanctions, military power, etc.). Beijing will view these as unacceptable efforts to contain China's rising power.

Both sides will see it to their advantage to make marginal concessions in pursuit of domestic priorities. Meanwhile, the U.S. and China will continue to move aggressively to decouple. A pair of Friday headlines: "China Allows Limited Exports of Rare Earths as Shortages Continue." "China issues rare earth licenses to suppliers of top 3 US automakers, sources say."

In the case of the three auto manufacturers, the granted export licenses were "temporary." Beijing will likely use rare earths and magnets to extract concessions on U.S. semiconductor and high-tech export controls. And we'll wait to see if President Trump plays tough or retreats again. I just have a difficult time envisaging the two nations mending relations. There will be ebbs and flows, but the rival superpowers face years of ongoing antipathy and escalating conflict.

Returning to the markets, it's an interesting juncture for "risk on" and loose financial conditions. Might TACO ("Trump Always Chickens Out") be more of a market "risk off" phenomenon? An emboldened (resolved to display fortitude) President may welcome testing China's resolve - of which they have plenty. On the subject of tests, it would not be surprising to see the bond market vigilantes make their presence known as budget reconciliation negotiations reach the final stretch. And the more that surging stocks foster complacency (on inflation, the budget, tariffs, China, trade wars…), the greater the pressure that befalls vulnerable Treasury and global bond markets.

Stocks can celebrate having arrived at the summer doldrums in one piece. But there's an awful lot that could go wrong.
