data.title: CAVA Group: Growth Momentum, But Brand And Valuation Questions Persist
data.description:
The CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA) is expanding at a good pace, maintaining healthy store level and overall metrics. However, there is a worry behind the growth story around the brand reflecting customer love, as same store sales are not established yet. Let dive in deeper into CAVA and see if the recent corrections have priced the negatives in, in an otherwise healthy growth story.

Fast Expansion but Brand Worries

The fast casual Mediterranean chain is aggressively expanding units. 66 units slated to open in 2025 corresponds to ~18% unit growth from 2024. The steady pace of expansion has seen a similar steady growth in revenue over the past few quarters. However, in double-clicking, I see same store sales growth healthy but dropping to 10.8% in Q1 2025. Not concerning, but shows new store sales or pricing hikes holding up revenue growth more than same store growth - a question on its brand health. 2025 full-year guidance also pegs same store growth at 7% at the midpoint. The novelty of new store openings (or price hikes) increasing topline in the short term is not a problem, and same store growth deceleration could be from spending cut headwinds as well.

On further drill down of its Q1 2025 quarterly report, a 7.5% traffic growth is observed, attributing a significant part of the same stores sales. The remaining 3.3% growth in topline is due to pricing and product mix benefits. CAVA's pricing strategy is not aggressive, with a commitment to keep prices flat for the remainder of the year. I will attribute the topline growth to new store novelty driving sales, increase in footprint in same stores, product mix changes (addons, etc.) and price hikes, in that order. While I do not see any systemic reason for the drop in comparable sales growth, it remains an area to keep an eye on as it is a key indicator of brand health. The economic impact of spending cuts, should not have been as severe given Brinker reported far healthier numbers in a similar period of macro worries.

Reasonable Margins

If price hikes are ruled out in the short term, margins become important to monitor. As food costs surge, and it is not passed on to customers, will margins be pressurized? Wage inflation remains a key concern. Food and packaging costs could remain pressurized directly and indirectly due to tariffs. And rental burdens are difficult to predict if expansion touches high street areas. So far, such cost inflation is not significant for CAVA. So gross margins are looking within acceptable range. EBITDA margins also look good and stable through CAVA's expansion.

Balance Sheet Health

The cash buffer looks healthy, but not enough to support rapid expansion plans for CAVA in the long term. Since historically, CAVA has turned to equity raises to fund the expansion, dilution risks exist in the future. The debt load will remain low, though. For immediate 2025 expansion plans and even beyond, current cash buffers and operating cash should be more than sufficient to cover for expansions. But eventually, margin improvements should be viewed as a positive development against equity dilution risks.

Near Term Consumer Outlook

Since the fundamental so far do not have anything that should drag share prices to the extent it has (~10% down in the past year and almost 50% down from the past year highs), the markets are pricing in significant consumer spending cuts to impact retail, especially food. I believe that CAVA's premium fast food positioning is still within the value overlap. There is likely to be an impact, but less than upscale dining, for example. At the same time, it will not be as immune as an out-and-out value brand like McDonalds or Taco Bell.

However, compensating from that caution is the added optionality of CAVA's foray into FMCG and packaged foods. This is a small fraction of the business today, but could be leveraged not only as a hedge against restaurant revenues, but as an independent growth story of its own.

Valuations Look Expensive

A look at the valuations show the forward EV to EBITDA ratio for CAVA towards the lower end of its historical spectrum, but still higher than Shake Shack and even Chipotle. I have kept a comparison with EAT because it is seeing great same store metrics supporting the brand story despite it being a completely different full service dining segment.

All in all, the valuations for CAVA do not look comfortable, despite it pricing in higher growth expectations. Particularly in the wake of discretionary spending cut threats. Although guidance does not reflect any major threat expected in 2025, and prices are also being kept on hold for the year. CAVA will certainly not do better than a Chipotle or Shake Shack in a stress scenario. Its premium, healthy and fresh placement has takers, but is also most likely to react to any economic slowdown.

Hold CAVA

CAVA is navigating a phase of rapid expansion which continues to support top-line momentum. However, the noticeable deceleration in same store sales growth from double digits to a projected 7% for the full-year signals caution around brand strength and organic demand. While new store openings and product mix enhancements are currently driving revenue, sustainable growth will depend on steadier same store traffic.

Margins remain stable despite inflationary pressures, and the company’s solid cash position and low debt provide financial flexibility. But reliance on equity raises to fund expansion introduces potential dilution risk unless margins improve operating cash flow. CAVA’s premium fast-casual also makes it vulnerable to demand issues in an environment of discretionary spending cuts. The FMCG packaged foods segment offers an attractive optionality, though still in its early stages.

Valuations reflect high-growth expectations, and not at the right time for a premium fast food brand like CAVA. OS, overall, I suggest a Hold for CAVA. There is merit in its growth story, but current valuations entering stress territory do not support fresh Buys.

data.title: Omada Health: Helping Patients, Investors As Well?
data.description:
Shares of Omada (NASDAQ:OMDA) have seen a successful public debut as investors like the accelerating pace of growth for this remote health firm which has a distinctive approach to aiding patients which suffer from chronic disease.

Investors like the growth and the positioning, and while I see potential as well, it is elevated sales multiples and continued losses, as well as quite some risks to sustain this growth story, which make it easy for me to take a wait-and-see approach here.

Bending The Curve In Diseases

Omada has a mission to bend the curve for various diseases ranging from obesity to diabetes, musculoskeletal disease and hypertension, among others. The company aims to set people up for exercise in a structure which triggers and creates sustained behavioral change.

Following a welcome kit, followed by a coaching video, connected devices, training, tracking of steps and meals, to achieving goals, the company creates a real journey for patients to meet their long-term goals.

This is a mission-led business founded in 20011, as founders Adrian James, Andrew DiMichele and Sean Duffy learned that patients typically obtained good advice when they visited their doctors, but enjoyed few recommendations and checks thereafter. At the same time, over 150 million Americans suffer from various chronic indications, overwhelming the healthcare system at large.

The company specifically focuses on this period of time, what it calls "between-care" to bring various healthcare professionals and clients together. Over time, the company has grown to have more than 2,000 customers and 679,000 enrollments as of the end of the first quarter. This is based on a potential patient population of more than 150 million US eligible patients, with some 18 million been covered by their insurance programs.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Omada aimed to sell 7.9 million shares in a preliminary price range between $18 and $20 per share, with final pricing set at $19 per share. With all shares sold by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $150 million, that is, of course, excluding the over-allotment option.

With nearly 56 million shares outstanding, the equity of the business is valued at $1.06 billion at the offer price. This actually includes a roughly $160 million net cash position on a pro forma basis, suggesting that operating assets are valued at around $900 million at the offer price.

The company generated nearly $123 million in sales in 2023 on which a substantial GAAP operating loss of $66 million was reported. Revenues rose by around 38% to $170 million in 2024 as GAAP operating losses narrowed to less than $44 million, with the achievement being noticeable, certainly on a relative basis.

It should be noted that growth accelerated a bit during the year, with fourth quarter sales up 47% on the year before to nearly $48 million, as operating losses narrowed to $7.1 million. Growth even accelerated to nearly 57% in the first quarter of this year, with sales coming in at $55 million, for a $220 million run rate. Quarterly operating losses increased a bit to $8.4 million, up on a sequential basis, but down substantially from this period last year. Note that the run rate of in terms of sales in relation to the current engagement rates comes in at just over $300 per year for every user, a reasonable amount, certainly in the healthcare space.

Shares have moved up to the $25 mark halfway the first day of trading, pushing up the valuation by some $336 million, to more than $1.2 billion. Based on the current revenue trend, this values the business at 5-6 times sales, fast-growing, but still lossmaking sales. Frankly, the acceleration in growth is comforting, also in the light of reduced losses, although that the trend of sequential narrowing operating losses was broken in the first quarter of 2025.

Concluding Thoughts

The truth is that I am passing on this opportunity. While growth is impressive at >50% rates and losses are coming down, there are real risks as well. This includes reliance on a few PBMs, concerns about continued engagement down the road (read churn) and privacy concerns.

There is somewhat of a peer in this market, although its business model is very different. This is Teladoc Health (TDOC) which has a different business model. Interestingly enough, its current equity valuation comes in at just $1.2 billion, following a huge boom-and-bust cycle during the pandemic, with the business valued at just around 0.5 times lossmaking sales.

All this makes me cautious, making that I take a wait-and-see approach here. While accelerating growth is welcomed, the lack of operating leverage in the most recent quarter, on top of the most sustainable concerns mentioned above, make it easy for me to take a pass here.

data.title: Torrid Holdings: The Picture Is Risky Given Management's Bold Changes
data.description:
One interesting company that seems to be going through a big transition right now is Torrid Holdings (NYSE:CURV), a direct-to-consumer retailer that focuses on providing clothing and accessories for what it calls ‘curvy women’ who are typically between 25 and 40 years old. Back in early December 2023, I came out as neutral on the business. Earnings were approaching and, leading up to that point, the company had exhibited decent growth but was showing signs of weakness. In particular, revenue had flat-lined and cash flows had started falling. I urged investors to exercise caution because of what the future might hold for the business.

Since then, things have not gone particularly well for the company. Fundamental results have continued to weaken and shares have risen by only 16% compared to the 31.7% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed. On June 5th, management announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue fell short of expectations, earnings per share did exceed analysts’ estimates. But with management now focused on shuttering many of its stores and shifting even more heavily toward a direct-to-consumer strategy that emphasizes e-commerce, there is a great deal of uncertainty that investors need to take into consideration. Add on top of this the risks posed by tariffs, and I believe that shareholders would be wise to consider this a risky prospect. Admittedly, this is one that has the potential for meaningful upside. But that's only if things go well. Until we see additional clarity on the matter, I think that taking a more cautious approach and rating the company a ‘sell’ makes sense.

A risky time

On June 5th, the management team at Torrid Holdings announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue came in at $266 million. That represents a decline of 4.9% compared to the $279.8 million the business reported one year earlier. Some of this drop can be attributed to a 3.5% decline in comparable store sales. Management also mentioned that the number of locations that they have in operation contracted from 658 last year to 632 this year. At the end of the day, the revenue reported by the company ended up being $4.2 million lower than what analysts were hoping to see.

When it comes to the sales figures that the company reported, management mentioned in their first quarter earnings call that customers are remaining price and value conscious. Despite this pain, the firm mentioned that, after having a particularly challenging February, it saw improvements in March and additional signs of stabilization in April. It seems as though certain categories like dresses, denim, and non-denim bottoms performed well and that this reflected a positive consumer response to the refreshed assortments and ‘trend-right’ products that the company provided.

On the bottom line, the picture was a bit better compared to what was anticipated. Earnings per share did unfortunately plummet from $0.12 to $0.06. That took overall net profits down from $12.2 million to $5.9 million. However, the earnings per share figure reported by the company happened to be $0.02 above what analysts were hoping to see. The company benefited from a decline in interest expense from $9.4 million to $8.2 million. In addition to this, interest income, net of other income and expenses, saw a net shift in the company's favor of $0.8 million year over year.

Other profitability metrics also, without exception, worsened on a year-over-year basis. Operating cash flow, for instance, went from $27.6 million to negative $18 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $33.7 million to $23.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $38.2 million to $27.1 million. It stands to reason that lower revenue would bring with it decreased margins. This was especially the case as the firm's marketing expenses jumped from 4.58% of sales to 5.77%. When applied to the revenue generated for the most recent quarter, that alone impacted the company's pre-tax profits by $3.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the last three fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see the number of active customers that the company has had and the average revenue per active customer. Unfortunately, management does not provide these figures on a quarterly basis, so we don't know what the picture looked like in the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. But it is clear that the overall trend has been negative in recent years. It doesn't help that the number of stores in operation for the business has declined.

Management knows that they have a problem here. And to address these issues, they are taking some bold but risky steps. The firm has, over the last few years now, been working on closing down underperforming stores. But this year, they plan to reduce their physical footprint by up to 180 locations. Considering that the business ended 2024 with just 634 stores in operation, that marks a significant decline in its overall size. In most cases, this would justify an extreme degree of bearishness. However, the business does have one weapon at its disposal. This is the fact that, historically speaking, most of the company's revenue has come from its digital operations. In 2024, 61% of sales came from its e-commerce business. And during the first quarter, according to management, underperformance of its stores relative to its websites has pushed this share of total revenue up to nearly 70%. The objective here is to decrease what are currently significant fixed costs.

Conceptually, this change makes a lot of sense. I can also understand why investors might be bullish about this move. It certainly could pay off well. Having said that, I would feel a lot better about the situation if the business had a track record of growing its online sales. Management has not provided a complete breakdown of online revenue. Instead, they only give approximate percentages. But if we take those percentages to heart, we would see that, from 2022 through 2024, e-commerce sales for the business contracted from $785.8 million to $673.3 million.

Despite this unfavorable track record, management seems optimistic about the upcoming launches of Lovesick and Studio Luxe. These are part of its sub-brand strategy, which consists of differently branded offerings that cater to diverse fashion preferences. Examples of successful ones in the past have included Festi, Nightfall, Belle Isle, and Retro Chic. Because of the success of these brands before, with them significantly outperforming other offerings provided by the enterprise, management is targeting for its sub-brands to account for almost one-third of their revenue by next year. There is some hope that this could help to turn the ship around. But there are also other risks as well.

For starters, because of the trade war situation that is going on, the company anticipates a net tariff impact for the year of $20 million. However, they said that that will be fully offset by discretionary cost reductions, and other changes like shrinking their physical footprint. Keep in mind that this net impact is after factoring in other changes like potential price increases, changes in sourcing, and more. In response to the tariffs, the company has worked quickly to reduce exposure to China sourced goods, such that they will be in the low single digit range for the rest of this year. That's down from the mid-teens range leading up to this point. Because all of the company’s shoes are imported from China, the firm has made the decision to ‘temporarily pause and re-evaluate’ its shoe offerings. The current expectation is that the company will eventually re-enter this category, but for 2025, they expect this decision to negatively affect revenue by between $40 million and $45 million.

Keeping these issues in mind, the firm does anticipate that revenue this year will be between $1.03 billion and $1.06 billion. At the midpoint, this would represent a drop of 5.5% compared to the $1.10 billion generated for 2024. Management also anticipates EBITDA of between $95 million and $105 million, compared to the $109.1 million reported for last year. Assuming that adjusted operating cash flow will fall at the same rate, this would mean a reading of $86.6 million for the year. That's down from $94.5 million last year.

Using these estimates, and using the historical results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Normally, these are multiples that I would be very bullish about. But because of all the changes going on and the uncertainty involved, I think that this prospect is too risky for me at the moment. In addition to this, there are other players in this industry that are trading cheaper than it is. In the table below, you can see how the firm stacks up against five similar enterprises. Three of the five are cheaper than it is on this basis.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Torrid Holdings 6.0 7.8
Stitch Fix (SFIX) 20.7 60.2
Genesco (GCO) 2.5 6.0
Zumiez (ZUMZ) 10.6 4.1
J.Jill (JILL) 3.9 3.1
The Children's Place (PLCE) 1.0 21.3

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that while Torrid Holdings certainly has potential if things go well, there are too many risk factors at play to make me feel comfortable. Until we see some meaningful signs of stabilization and understand the full impact that these store closings will have on the business, I think that down grading it to a soft ‘sell’ is prudent.

data.title: Why Everyone's Talking About Private Credit In 2025
data.description:
As markets adjust to new tariff policies, private credit is gaining traction. VanEck shares insights on why this asset class is in sharp focus.

Demand for private credit continues to surge, with projections suggesting it could hit $2.8 trillion by 2028.1 Private credit has stepped into the spotlight in 2025, and it’s not hard to see why. Amid volatile markets, evolving trade dynamics, and investors’ growing appetite for alternative income, private credit offers something rare: the potential for attractive risk-adjusted returns and yield. Here’s what’s fueling the boom and why more investors are adding it to their portfolios.

What’s Driving the Private Credit Boom?

The 2023 regional banking crisis accelerated the pre-existing trend of banks retreating from lending to private companies that tend to be below investment grade or not rated. This void is being filled by private credit. Unlike banks, which are subject to stricter regulations and capital requirements, private lenders offer more flexibility and can tailor loan structures to meet the specific needs of borrowers. This flexibility, coupled with their increasing pool of capital positions private credit as a crucial source of financing, particularly for businesses that may not meet the stricter criteria of traditional banks.

More recently, the macro backdrop of 2025 has been a perfect catalyst for private credit’s rise. Volatility across traditional stocks and bonds has prompted investors to seek for assets less tied to public market swings. At the same time, geopolitical tensions and new tariff policies are reshaping global trade, tightening corporate borrowing conditions, and creating demand for non-bank lending alternatives.

Private Credit 2025 Outlook

The momentum behind private credit isn’t expected to slow. In 2025, specifically, there are several tailwinds for private credit:

  • Middle-market companies often underserved by traditional banks are increasingly turning to private lenders to finance growth, acquisitions, and recapitalizations.
  • Sector-specific opportunities in industries like healthcare, technology, and infrastructure are expanding.
  • Institutional allocations continue to grow, with pension funds and insurers viewing private credit as a core income strategy rather than a niche alternative.

However, investors should also be mindful of risks: tighter financial conditions could pressure weaker borrowers, and while the market is expanding, manager selection will remain critical.

Why Investors Are Paying Attention to Private Credit

A sustained spread premium continues to exist between public and private markets, allowing investors to earn higher yields for lending privately. This spread advantage, combined with the evolving debt landscape, has created a rich opportunity set for private credit lenders.

Recent years have seen a major shift: instead of relying on traditional syndicated loan markets, many companies have turned to private credit for refinancing needs. Looking ahead, attention is shifting toward a looming maturity wall, as a significant portion of high-yield bonds and leveraged loans are set to come due in 2026 and 2027. In contrast to past cycles, this maturing debt could see even greater private market participation as companies seek flexible financing solutions outside of traditional bank lending.

At the heart of private credit’s appeal are three core benefits:

  • Attractive yields: Private loans often offer higher income than comparable public bonds, a compelling advantage in a higher-rate world.
  • Reduced correlation to traditional stocks and bonds: Private credit tends to be less sensitive to day-to-day market swings, helping diversify portfolios.
  • Potential inflation protection: Many private credit deals feature floating-rate structures that adjust with rising rates.

Access to private credit is expanding. Historically reserved for large institutions, private credit is now increasingly available to individual investors through new vehicles, such as ETFs. This democratization is reshaping the landscape, but with it comes the need for careful attention to credit quality, illiquidity risks, and manager expertise.

The Role of Private Credit in a Diversified Portfolio

Is private credit here to stay? All signs point to yes.

The rise of private credit is part of a broader rethinking of portfolio construction. As traditional fixed income struggles to deliver the same returns and diversification benefits it once did, investors are seeking alternative income sources. Private credit fits the bill, offering a differentiated return stream while complementing traditional assets and investment strategies.

Institutions are leading the way, often carving out dedicated allocations to private credit within their multi-asset portfolios. Retail investors are following suit, thanks to more accessible vehicles. VanEck believes that private credit will play a strategic role in diversified portfolios moving forward. However, investors should carefully weigh liquidity needs, risk tolerance, and manager quality when making allocation decisions.

Accessing the Market: Private Credit ETFs and BDCs

Traditional private credit investments, while offering the potential for attractive returns and yield, come with a drawback: illiquidity. Unlike publicly traded stocks or bonds, these investments often involve long lock-up periods, typically several years. This means your money is tied up for the duration, inaccessible for immediate needs or strategic portfolio adjustments. This inflexibility can be a major hurdle for investors who require more dynamic access to their capital, especially in a market rife with uncertainties. In an environment where liquidity is highly prized, Business Development Companies (BDCs) present a compelling, liquid alternative to traditional private credit strategies.

BDCs bridge the gap between traditional private credit and publicly traded securities. BDCs offer the same benefits as traditional private credit strategies – the potential for higher yields and diversification away from traditional stocks and bonds, but in a much more liquid form.

ETFs like VanEck’s BDC Income ETF (BIZD) offer diversified exposure to leading BDCs, which in turn invest across a wide range of private loans. For investors seeking access to private credit opportunities without the barriers of direct lending, including capital commitments, illiquidity, and operational complexity, BIZD provides a streamlined alternative.

Importantly, these vehicles allow investors to participate in private credit’s growth potential while maintaining daily liquidity, transparency, and simplicity.

Investing in the Firms Powering Private Markets

Another way to tap into the growth of private credit is by investing in the companies that operate and manage the funds behind it. These are alternative asset managers, firms that provide financing through private credit while also overseeing capital across private equity, real estate, infrastructure, and venture capital. As the organizations sourcing deals, structuring loans, and allocating private capital, they are positioned to benefit directly from the increasing demand for private market solutions.

While investors have long been able to buy shares of publicly listed alternative asset managers, the role these firms play in portfolios is evolving. As demand for private credit and other private market strategies grows, these companies are expanding their business models. Many are reaching beyond institutional capital and into wealth management and retirement channels, broadening their investor base and solidifying their importance in today’s investment landscape. With access to long-term capital and resilient fee structures, they are well positioned to benefit from the continued shift toward private markets.

To help investors access this opportunity, VanEck has introduced the Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ). This ETF seeks to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of the MarketVector Alternative Asset Managers Index (MVAALTTR), which is intended to track the overall performance of alternative asset managers across private equity, venture capital, private credit, private real estate, and private infrastructure.

Unlike direct private investments, GPZ offers diversified exposure to the firms driving the expansion of private markets. As private markets reshape global capital flows, alternative asset managers have become central to this transformation. GPZ gives investors a way to participate in that growth through a single, liquid, and transparent ETF.

What Comes Next for Private Credit?

As the private credit market matures, several themes are likely to shape its future:

  • Regulation: Increased attention from regulators may lead to more oversight, especially around risk management and disclosure.
  • Growing demand: New entrants, from large asset managers to fintech platforms, are making private credit more competitive and innovative.
  • Portfolio integration: Private credit is becoming a core portfolio allocation, not just a niche play.

Looking ahead, VanEck sees private credit evolving into a mainstream income strategy for both institutions and individual investors. The combination of structural growth drivers, macro tailwinds, and new access pathways positions private credit as a critical part of the investing conversation — not just in 2025 but well beyond.

While the BDCs that comprise the Index are expected to generate income in the form of dividends, certain BDCs during certain periods of time may not generate such income. The Fund will indirectly bear its proportionate share of any management fees and other operating expenses incurred by the BDCs and of any performance-based or incentive fees payable by the BDCs in which it invests, in addition to the expenses paid by the Fund. A BDC’s incentive fee may be very high, vary from year to year and be payable even if the value of the BDC’s portfolio declines in a given time period.
